title: $1,000 in S&P 500 at the 2009 Market Bottom
description: What $1,000 invested in the S&P 500 (via SPY ETF) on March 9, 2009 — the exact bottom of the 2008-2009 financial crisis — would be worth today with dividends reinvested. The S&P 500 had fallen 57% from its October 2007 peak. Buying at the bottom required extreme conviction.
dividend_note: SPY pays quarterly dividends. DRIP assumed throughout. Dividends meaningfully compound over a 16-year holding period.
benchmark_note: For context: $1,000 invested at the October 2007 peak (before the crisis) would still be worth ~$7,200 today with dividends reinvested — a 620% gain over 18 years (~11.7% CAGR).
educational_note: Nobody knew March 9, 2009 was 'the bottom' in real time. Headlines were catastrophic. Banks were being nationalized. This scenario illustrates why staying invested — or investing more — during extreme market fear can be extraordinarily rewarding.
